band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can notice what my body is telling me, name a feeling, and use one calming strategy to return to learning.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My feelings have names, and naming them helps me notice them.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify a common personal trigger and choose a practised strategy to manage my response.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: What I feel is often set off by something specific, and I can learn what sets me off.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can apply a chosen strategy in a challenging situation and reflect on how well it worked.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: A strategy either works for me or doesn't depending on the situation, and I can tell by checking afterwards.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can apply my strategies under stress across different settings, explain what worked and what didn't, and state one change for next time.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: How I regulate depends on what's available to me in a given context, and adjusting my approach across settings is how regulation gets more reliable.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can independently sustain my regulation strategies through sustained high-pressure periods, adapt them proactively rather than reactively, and reflect on how my regulation affects others around me.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Regulation is not only a personal tool — my emotional state shapes the people around me, and taking responsibility for that is part of mature self-regulation.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can articulate my personal regulation system — the strategies I rely on, the conditions that disrupt it, and the adaptations I have built over time — and support others in developing their own.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: A mature regulation system is one I can describe and teach, not merely use — and the capacity to support others' regulation deepens my own.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can name a feeling another person might be having and offer a kind word or a specific helpful action.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Other people have feelings too, and I can sometimes tell what they are.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can describe a situation from another person's perspective and ask if they would like help before acting.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: The same situation can look different to different people, and asking is more reliable than guessing.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can notice what others say and do in a group, check in when I'm unsure, and adjust my actions to match their stated needs or boundaries.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Other people signal what they need both in what they say and in what they do, and paying attention to both makes me more responsive.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can repair a relationship after conflict by listening, owning my part, and agreeing on a next step that works for both people.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Conflict is a normal part of relationships, and repair is a specific skill that matters more than avoiding conflict in the first place.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can facilitate repair in a group setting, naming the group dynamics that contributed to conflict and proposing steps that address the needs of multiple parties.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Conflict in groups is shaped by more than individual feelings — group norms, histories, and power dynamics all affect what is possible in repair, and skilled empathy accounts for all of these.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can recognise when structural or systemic factors are shaping someone's experience and adjust my empathic response accordingly, including advocating for a systemic change when individual repair is insufficient.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Full empathic understanding requires knowing what shapes a person's context, not only what they say they feel — and sometimes the most empathic response is to address the conditions, not just the person.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can use my senses to attend to one task for a short time and describe how I brought my attention back when it wandered.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My attention wanders, and I can bring it back.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can use a practised attention strategy during a task and describe what made it easier or harder to focus.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Attention is affected by what's going on around me and inside me, and some conditions help more than others.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can evaluate how an attention strategy affected my focus during a specific task and explain why it did or didn't help.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Strategies work through specific mechanisms, and knowing the mechanism helps me choose the right strategy for the situation.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can sustain attention during a challenging task, identify what is pulling it away, and use a deliberate strategy to return.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: The things that pull my attention away are often more informative about what I need than about the task itself.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can design the conditions for sustained attention in my own work — including managing digital distractions — and evaluate how different attentional strategies interact with different types of tasks.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Attention management is an environment-design problem as much as a personal-discipline problem, and my physical and digital environment shapes what my attention can do.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can audit my attentional patterns across high-stakes work over a sustained period, identify the conditions and strategies that most reliably support my best work, and make deliberate adjustments to my workflow to protect them.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Attention is a limited resource that must be strategically protected, not just reactively managed — and knowing how my best attention works is a professional and personal asset.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can retell a short experience and state one thing I learned from it.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: I can learn from what I did.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can stop and state at least one helpful and one harmful possible outcome before making a choice.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Most choices have upsides and downsides, and thinking about both before deciding gives me a better chance of choosing well.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can compare multiple options, state my choice, and give a reason based on my values.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Good decisions consider more than one option and are grounded in what matters to me, not just what's easiest or most obvious.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can explain the trade-offs in a decision and justify my choice by considering how it affects different people.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Decisions rarely have a single right answer, and justifying a choice requires being honest about what I'm trading off and for whom.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify the biases and assumptions likely to be affecting my reasoning in a decision, adjust my process to reduce their impact, and evaluate a decision framework by the quality of its reasoning, not its outcome.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: The process that produces a decision matters as much as the decision itself — good reasoning under uncertainty is evaluable even before the outcome is known.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can construct a reasoned position on a complex, contested, and high-stakes decision — weighing multiple types of evidence, acknowledging irreducible uncertainty, and making the logic of my choice explicit while remaining open to revision.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Mature decision-making is not certainty — it is the ability to act wisely under uncertainty while maintaining intellectual honesty about what I do not know and openness to changing my view.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can name my body's basic needs and practise simple personal hygiene routines with reminders.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body needs specific things to feel well.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can explain how sleep, food, and movement affect how I feel, and set a goal for one healthy habit.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: How I feel day-to-day is shaped by what I do consistently, not just once.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can track one habit for a week, identify a barrier, and explain why I want to keep or change it.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Habits break down for specific reasons, and noticing the reason is the first step in adjusting the habit.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can compare two sources of health information, create a balanced weekly habits plan, and adjust it based on logged results.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Designing for my life means planning for the real conditions I live in, including the ones that disrupt me, rather than an idealised version of myself.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify the structural and social factors that affect my access to healthy habits, evaluate health claims using evidence-quality criteria, and redesign my habits plan to account for conditions I cannot control individually.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Health habits sit inside a social and structural context that shapes what is possible for me — and honest health literacy means understanding both what I can change personally and what requires systemic conditions I do not control alone.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can design a health maintenance approach that is sustainable across the demands of adult life — accounting for competing priorities, social pressures, and systems-level constraints — and articulate why sustainable habits require external conditions, not only personal willpower.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Long-term health is not a product of willpower alone — it is enabled or disabled by the environments, relationships, and systems surrounding individuals, and understanding this is what makes health literacy genuinely critical.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can name a trusted adult I can ask for help and practise a simple self-care routine.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Asking for help is a normal part of taking care of myself.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify my body's early warning signs and choose a helpful self-care response.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body gives me signals before I'm overwhelmed, and responding early is more effective than responding late.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can make a self-care plan that works at home and school and list trusted people or services for support.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Self-care has to work in the actual conditions of my life, and knowing who to turn to is as important as knowing what to do for myself.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can maintain my key self-care routines during stressful times and describe one way I support a peer to do the same.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Self-care practices are most valuable precisely when they're hardest to do, and helping others maintain theirs strengthens my own.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can maintain my self-care practices through extended periods of difficulty without requiring external prompts, seek help appropriately when my own strategies are insufficient, and model self-care norms in my peer group without making it performative.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Sustainable resilience is grounded in a realistic view of my own limits — knowing when to extend myself and when to rest is as important as the ability to persevere.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can construct a personal resilience framework — including my non-negotiable self-care commitments, my early-warning signals, and my recovery protocols — and articulate how this framework has developed across my years at school.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Resilience is not a trait I either have or lack — it is a system I have built, which I can describe, maintain, and continue to develop in adult life.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can name private body parts correctly and say who my trusted adults are that help keep me safe.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body is mine, and there are people who help keep me safe.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can set and respect boundaries, ask for clear consent, and seek help if I feel pressured.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Everyone has a right to say yes or no, and healthy relationships honour that every time.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can apply consent skills in non-sexual and online contexts and state my boundaries clearly to peers.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Consent applies in every kind of interaction, not only intimate ones, and it shows up in everyday choices.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can recognise power or peer-pressure risks, state my refusal and exit plan, and describe how I would support a peer to access help.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Power dynamics and pressure affect what feels possible to say, and recognising them is part of being able to refuse, exit, or help someone else do either.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can analyse a situation for coercion, grooming, or exploitation risk — including online and institutional contexts — apply legal knowledge of consent thresholds, and support someone else in understanding their rights and options without re-traumatising them.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Consent exists in legal, ethical, and relational dimensions that do not always align — understanding where they differ, and whose interests are protected in each, is part of sophisticated consent literacy.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can facilitate a discussion about consent, power, and boundaries with peers or younger students in a way that is age-appropriate, non-judgmental, and grounded in rights and evidence — including surfacing common myths and correcting them.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Consent education is not a one-time lesson but an ongoing cultural practice — and people who understand it well are positioned to model, teach, and protect it in the communities they belong to.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: N/A — not applicable at this developmental stage.
I can explain the basic changes of puberty, use personal hygiene routines, and find reliable health information from trusted sources.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body will change in predictable ways during puberty, and knowing what to expect makes the changes less confusing.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can manage my personal hygiene related to puberty, identify trusted sources of health information, and explain the basics of human reproduction.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body is changing in ways I can understand scientifically, and understanding the science is more useful than relying on rumour or fear.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can assess risks related to STIs, pregnancy, and substance use, explain protection methods, and identify steps to access confidential health advice.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Decisions about bodies and relationships involve real risks that can be understood and reduced, and knowing how to access help confidentially is part of being able to make informed choices.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can navigate healthcare systems relevant to my reproductive and sexual health — including understanding how to access confidential services, what rights I have as a young person, and how to evaluate health information in this domain against evidence-based guidelines.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My reproductive and sexual health is my own responsibility and right — accessing accurate information and appropriate support is not something I should need to depend on someone else to arrange for me.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can critically evaluate contested claims about sexual health, reproductive choices, and substance use against evidence — identify the ideological, cultural, or commercial interests shaping health discourse — and form and articulate a coherent, evidence-grounded personal position.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Sexual and reproductive health is a contested terrain where evidence, culture, politics, and personal values intersect — mature health literacy means being able to navigate this complexity without either uncritical acceptance or cynical dismissal of evidence.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can take turns in conversation, use kind words, and use an I-statement.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: How I talk to people affects how they feel.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can use I-statements to state my needs, ask about boundaries, and propose two options for a fair solution.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Being clear about what I need and curious about what others need makes working things out easier.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can listen in a disagreement without interrupting, choose words that stay respectful, and use a simple repair routine.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Staying in a conversation when I disagree is harder than avoiding it, and worth practising.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can state my boundaries clearly, paraphrase before I respond, and choose words that fit the situation including in conflict.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Communication quality matters most when stakes are high, and the same skills that work in calm conversations need to be deliberately applied in difficult ones.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can facilitate a structured conversation in a group setting where people disagree — managing the process rather than the positions, ensuring all voices are heard, and maintaining a constructive tone throughout.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Facilitating a group conversation under disagreement requires different skills than participating in one — the facilitator's job is to hold the process, not win the argument.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can communicate effectively across significant difference — cultural, generational, or positional — adjusting my register, tone, and frame without losing my own perspective, and navigating the power dynamics that shape who gets heard.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Communication across difference is not about giving up your own perspective — it is about understanding the conditions that make genuine dialogue possible and creating those conditions deliberately.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can do a classroom job and explain how it helps our class.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: I'm part of a group, and what I do makes a difference to it.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify when someone is left out and invite them to join an activity.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Belonging is something groups create on purpose, not something that just happens, and I can help create it.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify a need in our school, propose a simple project, and complete it with a team.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Making something happen in a community is a practice — noticing what's missing, proposing a response, and following through with others.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can work with a diverse group to address a need, show the impact of our project, and explain why the work matters to me.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: What I contribute to community is part of who I'm becoming, and working across difference strengthens what's possible.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can lead a sustained initiative that addresses a real community need, navigate the institutional and relational obstacles that arise, and articulate both the impact and the limits of what community action can achieve in this context.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Sustained community engagement requires understanding the systems that shape what is possible — and knowing those limits is not defeatism but honest civic literacy.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can articulate a clear sense of purpose — what I stand for, what I want to contribute, and why — grounded in my history of engagement, and make deliberate choices about the communities and roles through which I will pursue it beyond school.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Purpose is not discovered once — it is developed through accumulated engagement and reflection, and leaving school with a clear sense of it is one of the most significant outcomes of a wellbeing education.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can name body signals that tell me something about how I feel, and describe what sleep and food do for my body and brain.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body gives me information about how I feel before I think about it.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can explain what happens in my body during a fight, flight, or freeze response, and describe the three parts of the habit loop.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: My body's stress response happens automatically to protect me, and my habits run as a repeated loop.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can explain how the amygdala and prefrontal cortex interact during a stressful moment, explain what neuroplasticity means, and describe how the habit loop explains behaviour change at the level of mechanism.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Specific brain structures have specific functions, and my brain changes in response to what I practise.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can use accurate neuroscience vocabulary to explain how stress, emotion, attention, and habit interact as a system, and describe the role of the HPA axis in the stress response.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Stress, emotion, attention, and habit operate as an interdependent system, not as separate domains.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can apply my understanding of the stress-emotion-attention-habit system to evaluate a wellbeing intervention — explaining its proposed mechanism, what the evidence suggests about its effectiveness, and what it would and would not change.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Understanding a mechanism is useful only if you can apply it — and applying neuroscience means being able to evaluate claims made in its name, not just restate the system.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can critically evaluate a contested claim in wellbeing science — identifying where the evidence is strong, where it is uncertain, and where popular accounts have oversimplified or misrepresented the research — and form a reasoned position of my own.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Neuroscience is a developing field with unresolved debates, and the gap between research findings and popular application is often large — mature wellbeing literacy includes knowing where the science is solid, contested, or simply unknown.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify whether a health claim comes from a trusted adult or source and explain my reasoning.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Some people and sources know more about health than others, and I can ask.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can compare two health information sources and describe which one I trust more and why.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: A source's trustworthiness depends on where its information comes from.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can analyse a health claim by examining the type of evidence it uses and identifying whether the source has a reason to be biased.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Different types of evidence carry different weight, and the person making a health claim often has something at stake.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can evaluate a contested health claim by comparing the quality of evidence on each side, identifying the strongest and weakest arguments, and justifying my conclusion.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Health claims frequently contradict each other, and judging them well requires weighing quality of evidence rather than certainty of tone.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can assess the strength of an evidence base for a health claim — not just whether individual sources are trustworthy, but whether the cumulative research warrants the confidence placed in the claim — and identify the commercial or institutional interests that may have shaped the research agenda.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Health information is produced inside systems with funding pressures, commercial interests, and publication biases — and strong health literacy means understanding how knowledge is made, not just evaluating what it says.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can read and interpret a systematic review at an introductory level, trace how a policy recommendation was derived from an evidence base, and evaluate whether a specific guideline is well-supported or contested — articulating what I am certain of, uncertain about, and why.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: The distance from individual research finding to public health guideline is long and politically mediated — critical health literacy at the highest level means being able to trace that journey and evaluate it.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can describe one thing I noticed about how I reacted to a situation and say what I might do differently next time.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: I can think about how I did something, not just what I did.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can identify a pattern in how I tend to respond in a type of situation and give a reason for why that might happen.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Some of what I do isn't random — I tend to do similar things in similar situations, and that's useful information about me.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can analyse a pattern across more than one situation, explain what drives it, and describe a specific adjustment I have made or will make.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Patterns have drivers, and identifying the driver is what makes adjusting possible — noticing alone doesn't change much.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can evaluate a pattern in my thinking or responses across different contexts, justify what sustains it, and describe the effect of a specific adjustment I have made.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: What sustains a pattern is often different from what started it, and adjustments need to address the sustaining conditions to hold over time.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can apply a structured metacognitive protocol to a high-stakes academic or personal challenge — mapping my typical patterns, identifying the sustaining conditions, designing a specific intervention, tracking its effects, and iterating based on results.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Applying metacognition systematically to high-stakes work is different from reflecting generally — it requires being honest about patterns that are uncomfortable to see, and holding yourself accountable to the adjustments you commit to.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: I can construct a personal metacognitive framework — a set of strategies, prompts, and monitoring practices I have developed and tested — and articulate how I will apply it in the next phase of my learning and life beyond school.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Metacognitive self-direction is not just a school skill — it is the mechanism by which I continue to develop as a learner and person in any domain, and making it explicit is how I carry it forward.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: Stops and tries a different approach when the first attempt does not work, without being told to. After a setback, names what happened rather than dismissing it.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: When something doesn't work, trying differently is a choice I have.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: Refers back to a strategy they have previously found helpful, without being prompted. After a task, volunteers one specific observation about how they worked.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: What I've learned about myself becomes useful when I remember to use it.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: Identifies a pattern in how they tend to respond across more than one situation and names it spontaneously in conversation. Adjusts their approach mid-task in response to noticing something is not working.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Pattern-recognition about myself is most valuable when I catch it in the moment and adjust, not only when I reflect afterwards.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: Articulates a specific adjustment they have made to their thinking or behaviour and describes its effect across more than one domain. In novel or challenging situations, applies a self-awareness strategy without an adult prompt. Makes connections between wellbeing learning and behaviour in other contexts, unprompted.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Metacognitive self-direction is not a single habit but a disposition to treat myself as someone I'm learning about continuously.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: Consistently initiates metacognitive reflection without any external prompt across sustained periods of challenging work. When working with peers, naturally facilitates their self-reflection through questions rather than answers. Demonstrates stable self-directedness even when feedback challenges their self-assessment.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: Self-direction is not only about my own development — modelling it and supporting it in others is how a learning community is built, and doing so strengthens rather than depletes my own practice.

band_tagged_items.content_statement: Demonstrates a stable, articulated metacognitive identity — a consistent pattern of how they learn, reflect, and adjust — that they can describe to others and that persists across highly novel, high-stakes, or unfamiliar contexts without any scaffolding. Connects their wellbeing learning to their sense of who they are and who they are becoming, unprompted and with specificity.
band_tagged_items.understand_statement: By the end of Band F, metacognitive self-direction should be identity-deep — not a set of techniques I apply but a way of being in relation to my own growth that I take with me into any context.

skill_flags: internal_school_framework_explicit_bands: REAL's own framework has explicit A–F band structure. All items tagged with single-band high-confidence assignments. No ambiguity expected or present.

bands_ef_newly_authored: Bands E and F content was authored in this session (source-extended-ef.md) and has not yet been through REAL School teacher review. All 28 E/F items carry source_voice_preserved: true but represent new curriculum content, not source transcription.
lt_4_2_band_a_na: LT 4.2 Band A is intentionally N/A (not applicable at developmental stage). understand_statement is null for this item only.
